resume:
RHONDA CHASE-WILLIAMS
Sales Director, Enterprise SaaS | Atlanta, GA | rhonda.cw@email.com | (404) 555-0117

SUMMARY
Enterprise sales leader with fifteen years in B2B software, the last seven in second-line and director roles building and turning around field-sales teams. I have led organizations of front-line AEs and first-line managers carrying tens of millions in quota, owned a region's full forecast to the CRO, and personally rebuilt two underperforming teams. My orientation is operational: I run a tight inspection cadence, I coach managers to coach, and I am ruthless about pipeline hygiene because I have been burned by a happy-ears forecast before. I am at my best parachuting into a region that has missed plan and rebuilding the motion, the talent, and the discipline.

EXPERIENCE

ArborWorks Software (enterprise asset-management SaaS) — Sales Director, East
March 2021 to present, Atlanta
ArborWorks sells asset-management and field-service software to enterprise utilities, manufacturing, and transportation. I own the East region: four first-line managers and roughly thirty-two AEs, a regional quota in the mid-tens of millions, reporting to the CRO.
- Inherited a region that had missed plan for five straight quarters with a bloated, low-conversion pipeline and a demoralized bench.
- Rebuilt the management layer — coached up two of the four managers, transitioned one out, and hired one externally — and reset the deal-inspection cadence to a weekly forecast call plus a separate pipeline-generation review.
- Instituted a stricter stage-definition and exit-criteria framework so that 'commit' actually meant something; I personally inspected every deal over a set ACV threshold.
- Drove a multi-quarter return to plan; the region went from chronic misses to beating number, and pipeline coverage and forecast accuracy both improved materially.
- Partnered with marketing and SDR leadership to fix the top of the funnel, which had been over-indexed on low-fit inbound.
- Led the regional response to a competitive displacement threat when a well-funded entrant targeted three of our largest accounts.

Meridian Logistics Cloud (supply-chain SaaS) — Regional Sales Manager, then Senior RSM
January 2017 to February 2021, Atlanta and remote
Led first-line teams selling TMS and visibility software to mid-market and enterprise shippers.
- Managed a team of eight to ten AEs carrying a regional quota; promoted twice during my tenure.
- Built a repeatable account-planning rhythm for the team's top twenty named accounts.
- Coached two AEs from the bottom of the stack-rank into consistent quota attainment.
- Ran the region's response to a major pricing change without losing the top accounts.

HelixCRM (sales-tech SaaS) — Enterprise Account Executive
August 2010 to December 2016, Atlanta
Carried a full enterprise quota selling CRM and sales-engagement tooling to large B2B organizations.
- Consistently a top-third rep; President's Club in two of my last three years as an IC.
- Closed several seven-figure multi-year deals involving complex procurement and security review.
- Served as a peer mentor and interview panelist, which is how I discovered I wanted to lead.

WHAT I AM GOOD AT
- Turnarounds. I have twice taken a team that was missing and rebuilt the discipline, the talent, and the forecast. I am comfortable making the hard people calls.
- Forecast inspection. I run a tight cadence and I do not accept a number I cannot defend deal-by-deal to the CRO.
- Coaching managers. My leverage is through my first-line managers; I spend most of my time making them better coaches, not selling deals for them.
- Pipeline discipline. I am unforgiving about stage definitions and exit criteria because soft stages create phantom pipeline, and phantom pipeline is how a region ends up missing five quarters in a row without anyone seeing it coming.
- Reading a forecast roll-up. I have a good instinct for which manager is sandbagging and which is happy-ears, and I calibrate my inspection pressure accordingly instead of applying the same scrutiny to everyone.
- Cross-functional alignment under a number. When the top of the funnel is broken, I do not just complain to marketing; I sit in their planning and rebuild the SDR-to-AE definition of a qualified opportunity together.

WHAT I AM STILL WORKING ON
- Patience in the first 90 days. I sometimes move on a struggling manager faster than my own boss is comfortable with, and I am learning to bring leadership along.
- Strategic, multi-year account expansion. My instinct is the current-quarter number; I am developing the longer land-and-expand muscle.
- Channel and partner-led motions. My experience is overwhelmingly direct field sales; I have limited reps with a partner-sourced model.

TOOLS
Salesforce, Clari, Gong, Outreach, and a homegrown deal-scoring model I built with sales ops. I run my forecast in Clari but I pressure-test it in Gong.

EDUCATION
B.B.A., Marketing, University of Georgia, 2010.

A NOTE ON HOW I LEAD
I am the leader you bring in when a region is broken and the forecast can no longer be trusted. I rebuild from the inspection cadence up: clean stages, defensible commits, managers who coach, and a bench you can win with. I am direct, I am numbers-first, and I would rather deliver a smaller honest forecast than a big hopeful one. I am looking for a director or second-line seat with a turnaround to run or a team to scale with rigor.
jd:
Sales Director, Enterprise — Cascade Field Solutions (vertical SaaS, ~700 employees)

Cascade sells field-service and workforce software to enterprise utilities and infrastructure operators. We are hiring a Sales Director to own a region of four first-line managers and ~30 AEs against a mid-eight-figure quota. Our East region has underperformed for over a year, and we need a leader who can rebuild forecast discipline and team performance without burning the house down.

What we expect:
- A concrete turnaround story: a team that was missing, what you changed in cadence, talent, and pipeline, and the measurable result quarter over quarter.
- Command of forecast mechanics: stage definitions, exit criteria, coverage ratios, and how you make 'commit' trustworthy.
- Real people-leadership judgment — coaching up versus transitioning out a manager — with the reasoning behind specific calls.
- The ability to quantify your impact: attainment, forecast accuracy, ramp, and retention of A-players.
- Maturity in bringing your own leadership along on hard calls.

Reports to the CRO. Partners with marketing, SDR leadership, and sales operations. Atlanta preferred.

history.q: Rhonda, thanks for joining. Give me the shape of your current org at ArborWorks — managers, AEs, quota, and who you report to.
You inherited a region that had missed plan for five quarters. What did your diagnosis say was actually broken?
Walk me through the first thing you changed in the inspection cadence and why that one first.
You reset stage definitions and exit criteria. Give me a concrete example of a stage that was being abused and how you redefined it.
On the management layer — you coached up two, transitioned one, hired one. Tell me about the manager you transitioned out and how you reached that call.
How long did you give that manager before deciding, and what specifically did you try first?
You said the region returned to plan. Quarter by quarter, what did attainment look like from when you arrived to now?
What happened to forecast accuracy specifically — how far off was the commit number when you arrived versus today?
Tell me about the competitive displacement threat on your three largest accounts. What did you personally do?
How did those three accounts end up — did you retain all three, and on what terms?

Let's go deep on the turnaround as one story: the situation you inherited, your goal, the specific moves you made — and then the hard numbers on where the region landed, quarter over quarter, on attainment and forecast accuracy.

candidate_last_answer: Sure. The situation was a region that had missed five straight quarters — bloated pipeline, soft commits, a bench that had stopped believing it could win. My goal in the first two quarters was simple: make the forecast trustworthy again, then make it bigger. So here's what I did, step by step. Week one I split the single Frankenstein forecast call into two: a deal-inspection call where I personally walked every deal over 250k against hard exit criteria, and a separate pipeline-gen review so we stopped robbing future quarters to cover the current one. I rewrote the stage definitions — 'commit' now required a signed mutual action plan and a confirmed economic buyer, not a rep's gut. On talent, I spent six weeks riding along before I touched anyone, then coached up two managers, moved one out who couldn't make the shift to inspection-based coaching, and hired one externally from a competitor. And I rebuilt the SDR-to-AE handoff with marketing because half the inbound was low-fit. That's the playbook I ran, and honestly it's the same playbook I'd run here on day one.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: That's the playbook I ran, and honestly it's the same playbook I'd run here on day one

expected-yield: she gave a full situation/goal/action narrative but omitted the result — pin the quarter-by-quarter numbers she was explicitly asked for: attainment percentage each quarter from arrival to now, the commit-versus-actual forecast-accuracy gap before and after, and A-player retention, rather than restating the playbook
